title: Sunday, February 4
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## February 4, 2018 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

February 4, 2018 was the Fifth Sunday after Epiphany in the liturgical calendar. The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this Sunday would have included Isaiah 40:21-31, Psalm 147:1-11, 20c, 1 Corinthians 9:16-23, and Mark 1:29-39. The church was in the season of Ordinary Time, specifically in the post-Epiphany period before the beginning of Lent (which would commence on Ash Wednesday, February 14, 2018, just ten days away).

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in a period of relative stability following the 2015 General Convention's significant actions on marriage equality and inclusivity. The Diocese of Colorado, led by Bishop Robert O'Neill (who had served since 2008), was focused on routine diocesan ministry and pastoral care. No major Episcopal denominational controversies or bishop elections were occurring in this specific timeframe, though broader church discussions about sexuality and theology continued from previous years.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in the depths of winter with typical Front Range weather patterns. The previous week had seen standard Colorado winter conditions without major weather emergencies. Local news was focused on routine municipal governance and state legislative matters as the Colorado General Assembly was in session (January 9 - May 9, 2018). The Downtown Denver area around the Cathedral on Logan Street was preparing for early spring activities and events.

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The week before February 4 was dominated by fallout from the January 30 release of the controversial Nunes Memo regarding FBI surveillance of Trump campaign adviser Carter Page, creating intense partisan divisions in Congress. The Women's March anniversary (January 20) had recently concluded, maintaining focus on #MeToo momentum and sexual harassment scandals across media, politics, and entertainment. The Winter Olympics were about to open in PyeongChang, South Korea (February 9-25), generating significant American media attention and anticipation.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The lead-up to the PyeongChang Winter Olympics was the dominant international story, with security preparations and diplomatic significance in focus (North Korea's participation and South Korean hosting creating geopolitical interest). The Syrian civil war continued with ongoing violence, though major headlines from the immediate previous week were minimal. Turkish military operations in northwest Syria against Kurdish forces maintained regional tension in the Middle East.

CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in early 2018 was marked by heightened political polarization following a contentious 2016 election and first year of the Trump presidency. The #MeToo movement was at peak cultural prominence, fundamentally reshaping conversations about power and accountability across institutions. There was anticipatory excitement for the upcoming Winter Olympics, and popular culture was engaged with major film and television releases. For a Denver congregation, the cultural mood blended winter fatigue with emerging spring optimism, alongside national anxiety about political divisions.
